sections.heading: The Results
sections.text: Jake Candler came out swinging with a two-song strategy — submitting Good Lookin' by Dixon Dallas AND Bad Luck by Zach Top — and somehow the group rewarded him for both, handing him 18 points and a Week 1 lead that he absolutely did not earn through humility. Good Lookin' took Song of the Week with 12 points, which is either a genuine endorsement of Dixon Dallas or a referendum on how easy this crowd is to please with a cowboy hat and a mustache.

sections.heading: The Chaos
sections.text: Kennon Stockwell had the most democratic song of the week — Fuck You Bitch by Wheeler Walker Jr. received votes from 7 unique people, more than any other song. Make of that what you will about the emotional state of this group. Kennon also submitted Everybody Needs Love by Drive-By Truckers in what can only be described as an emotional range that should concern the people closest to him.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text: Giles Geddes, Kevin Doran, and Will Balch all forgot to vote — or perhaps they were too busy going through their own divorces — and left a combined 21 points on the table. The Missed Points Board does not forget. It does not forgive. Henry Loong submitted Before He Cheats by Carrie Underwood and received zero points for it. Zero. Carrie Underwood has a Grammy. Multiple Grammys. Think about what you've done.

sections.heading: The Lesson
sections.text: Brooks Keuster submitted two genuinely good songs, got 0 points for both because he didn't vote, and is currently tied for last place with people who submitted nothing. Rough week, Brooks. See you all for Week 2. Vote or your songs die in silence.

rounds.name: Songs to Listen to While Daily Driving a Hearse on a Beautiful Day
rounds.description: It’s a perfect blue-sky day, windows down, light breeze—and you’re cruising in a hearse like it’s just another commute. There’s something weirdly peaceful about it, but also just off enough to make people do a double take at stoplights. This is the soundtrack for that contrast: bright, smooth, almost carefree music with an undercurrent of irony. Lean 70s soft rock / yacht rock / breezy indie / something more on brand for a hearse owner—something that feels like a Sunday drive, just… not in the vehicle anyone expected.

sections.heading: The Winner
sections.text: Mills T. understood the assignment better than anyone. Wakin on a Pretty Day by Kurt Vile took Song of the Week with 13 points and 7 unique voters — a dreamy, sun-drenched, 9-minute sprawl that sounds exactly like what you'd hear leaking out of a hearse doing 5 under the speed limit on a back road through Decatur. Mills went from 7th place last week to winning the whole round. That's a +13 point swing, which is the kind of momentum that makes Jake nervous.

sections.heading: Song Commentary
sections.text: Kennon submitted Jesus Was Way Cool by King Missile and got 12 points for it. If you don't know this song, it is exactly what it sounds like. It is a spoken word piece about Jesus being way cool. It is perfect for a hearse. Kennon gets it. Jake is still in first place overall at 32 points, but the gap is closing. Thunder Island by Jay Ferguson was the right vibe but only pulled 8 points.

sections.heading: The Debuts
sections.text: Hunter A. was a ghost in Week 1 — no submission, no votes, invisible. This week he submitted The Funeral by Band of Horses and Where'd All the Time Go? by Dr. Dog, pulled 15 combined points, and landed 3rd for the week. That's the single best debut performance of the season. Giles G. had a similar resurrection. Left 7 points on the table last week by not voting. This week he submitted Velvet Underground and pulled 13 points. Voting works, Giles.

sections.heading: The Fallers
sections.text: Jack A. fell off a cliff. From 9 points and a top-4 finish in Week 1 to 3 points in Week 2. That's a -6 point swing, the worst in the league. The Wall of Shame is empty this week — everyone who submitted also voted. The threat of public accountability appears to be working. But only 16 of 25 members submitted. The hearse has room.

rounds.name: Songs to Listen to When You Realize You Just Lost Your Second Adult Tooth of the Year and it Isn’t Even April Yet
rounds.description: You’re at the sink again, staring at something that absolutely shouldn’t have come out, doing the math and realizing this is the second time—and it’s not even April. This isn’t a fluke anymore, it’s a pattern. There’s a quiet, creeping dread that your body is starting to keep score, and you’re losing. This is the soundtrack for that moment where denial starts slipping into acceptance—uneasy, slightly unhinged, and darker than you want to admit. Lean moody indie, slow-burn alt rock, or stripped-down acoustic from the 2000s–2010s—songs that feel like something’s wrong, even if no one’s saying it out loud.

sections.heading: Scouting Report (written blind)
sections.text:
Who let the youth pastor in? Someone submitted Awake and Alive by Skillet AND Blow Me Away by Breaking Benjamin. These are the songs that play during the slow-motion montage in a Christian action film. Someone has a Monster Energy sticker on their laptop.

Someone submitted Creep by Radiohead. In 2026. This is the "I have a great personality" of song submissions.

Cotton Eye Joe — where did it come from? Where did it go? Where did it come from, Cotton Eye Tooth?

Hammer Smashed Face by Cannibal Corpse. The theme said "lost a tooth," not "had your skull caved in by a medieval weapon."

A 12-minute of Montreal deep cut submitted to a group that gave Dixon Dallas Song of the Week. This person was going to get zero votes and feel superior about it.

sections.heading: The Reveals
sections.text:
Emory L. detonated. First appearance of the season: 23 points. That's the highest single-week score anyone has posted. Hurt by Johnny Cash (13 pts) and Pulling Teeth by Green Day (10 pts) — one universally respected pick and one perfect title-to-theme hit. Emory showed up, chose violence, and left.

Coates C. walked in and won Song of the Week. Grillz by Nelly — 14 points, 8 voters. The new guy. First round. Coates just has it.

sections.heading: The Standings Shift
sections.text:
Mills T. is your new season leader at 42 points. Through The Wire by Kanye pulled 11 points — the most thematically perfect pick on the playlist (Kanye recorded it with his jaw wired shut).

Dray E. climbed to 2nd overall with 17 points. Widespread Panic's All Time Low and Warren Zevon's My Shit's Fucked Up are both deep cuts that people actually listened to. But All Time Low got 9 voters and only 9 points — the textbook snub. Everyone acknowledged it, nobody committed.

Jake C. posted a zero. Didn't submit. The Week 1 champion fell from 1st to 4th in one round.

sections.heading: The Unmasking
sections.text:
The youth pastor was Hilton H. Skillet AND Breaking Benjamin. Combined 1 point. Hilton, I'm not mad, I'm just disappointed.

Jack A. submitted Cannibal Corpse. Hammer Smashed Face got zero votes, because of course it did.

TR C. submitted Creep. The identity of the Creep submitter is now public record.

sections.heading: The Wall of Shame
sections.text: Will B., Henry L., and Brooks K. all submitted but didn't vote. Brooks submitted I Hate Myself And Want To Die by Nirvana — 9 points from 9 voters — and lost all of it. Three rounds in a row with zero credited points. Brooks. Please.

sections.heading: Prediction Accuracy
sections.text: Submitter guesses: 1 for 6. Only got Schuyler M. and Where Is My Mind. Vote predictions: had Through The Wire winning (it came 3rd), Grillz at 4th (it won). The model needs work. The dental humor was stronger than expected.
